english: can (can) (— well!); da ni (da ni), sṅar ni (sngar ni), ₒdi-las ni ('di las ni), sṅon-ċad ni (sngon chad ni) etc.; ṡiṅ-mkhan ni (shing mkhan ni) now, as to the carpenter, he . . . Dzl.; dár-ba ni (dar ba ni) now, with respect to the propagation (of the doctrine). In a similar manner it is frq. used, where we begin a new paragraph, heading it with its principal contents. In col. language the word before ni (ni) is rendered still more emphatic by repeating it once more after ni (ni): *zer ni zer dug* (zer ni zer dug) W. (it is true) they say so; *ḍi ni ḍi-te yoḍ* (di ni di te yod) it has been written, (to be sure); *jhé ni jhé* (jhe ni jhe) C., *ćo ni ćo dug* (co ni co dug) W. (certainly) they are working at it, (but . . .). In metrical compositions, esp. in mnemonic verses, it is often added as a mere metrical expletive, without any meaning, esp. after daṅ (dang). — 2. Ts.: demonstrative pron., *ri ni-ḷe ni tho-wa dug* (ri ni le ni tho wa dug) this mountain is higher than that. II. num. figure: 42.
(Hindi नील blue) 1. Cs. indigo. — 2. W. the blue pheasant of the South Himalaya, manāl.
(Hindi; Shaksp.: 'from the Portuguese leilam') auction, public sale.
1. col. for ni (ni). 2. for rnyiṅ? (rnying?) v. na-niṅ (na ning), że-niṅ (zhe ning).
n. of a plant, Melia Azedarachta.
Sch.: the great buzzard or mouse-hawk (?).
num. fig.: 72.
pf. and imp. nus (nus), to suck Cs., nu(-ba)-po (nu ba po), mo (mo), a suckling Cs., nu-kúg (nu kug) sucking-bag.
resp. ɣcúṅ-po (gcung po), W. *no* (no), a man's younger brother B. and C.

Cs. also čáb-nu (chab nu) (resp.?), breast, as two correspondent parts of the body, 1. mammary gland, female breast, bosom S.g. — 2. nipple, teat, also of males. — 3. dug, nipple of a cow's udder; nu-kyim (nu kyim), -gdan (-gdan), -bur (-bur), -bor (-bor), Cs. id. — nú-ṡa (nu sha) the thoracic muscle. — nu-rtsé (nu rtse), nu-sór (nu sor) Cs. the tip of the breasts, nipple. — nú-żo (nu zho) mother's milk, mai nú-żo (ma'i nu zho) Dzl.; nú-żo snún-par byéd-pa (nu zho snun par byed pa) to suckle, to give suck, Lt.; nú-żo skám-na (nu zho skam na) if she has no milk Lt.
1. W. *nó-mo* (no mo), the younger sister of a female, B. and col. — 2. v. nú-ba (nu ba).
(pronounced *nug-te* (nug te)) Ts., so, thus.
to suckle, W.: *pi-pi nud toṅ* (pi pi nud tong) give to suck! (= snún-pa (snun pa)).

1. the west, nub-(kyi) ṗyogs(-rol) (nub kyi phyogs rol) id.; núb-ṗyogs-su (nub phyogs su) towards the west; nub-byáṅ (nub byang) north-west; núb-kyi (nub kyi) of the west, western; v. also bdé-ba-ċan (bde ba can). — 2. evening, do-núb (do nub) this evening, to-night.

1. vb., to fall gradually, to sink, mtil-la (mthil la) to the bottom; to sink in, pús-mo núb-pa tsam (pus mo nub pa tsam) knee-deep Dzl. frq.; to go down, to set, of the sun, moon, frq.; fig. to decay, decline, of religion; núb-par gyúr-ba (nub par gyur ba) id.; núb-par byéd-pa (nub par byed pa) Sch. = vb. a. snúb-pa (snub pa). — 2. sbst. an inhabitant of the West.
evening; in the evening, frq.; nub gráṅ-gi (nub grang gi) happening every evening Sch.
W. col. for mun (mun).
denotes the form of the embryo in the second week: oval, oblong; mér-mer-po (mer mer po) id.

(cf brnúr-ba (brnur ba), snúr-ba (snur ba)), 1. to change place or posture, to move a little, *rig-te nur* (rig te nur) (v. sgrig-pa (sgrig pa)) W. move a little nearer together, stand or sit a little closer! núr-gyis tén-pa (nur gyis 'then pa) to pull gradually, to give short pulls Glr.; ṗa-bóṅ ₒdam rdzis-pa bżiṅ-du nur (pha bong 'dam rdzis pa bzhin du nur) the rock yielded, i.e. received impressions, like foot-prints on soft clay, Mil.; to step aside, to draw or fall back; to get out of its place, to be dislocated; *ṗi núr-la ḍúl-ċė, ṗi-log-la núr-ċė* (phi nur la dul ce, phi log la nur ce) W. to move slowly back. — 2. to crumble to pieces, Mil. of mountains during an unearthly storm, according to some Lamas, cf. snúr-ba (snur ba). — 3. Cs.: to approach, to come near to (?), yet cf. snúr-ba (snur ba).
I. 1. vb. to be able, to have sufficient moral or physical power,
